_note: Worked example: Harborline Provisions, a fictional regional foodservice distributor. This spec is the primary teaching artifact for the build engine — it exercises shorthand generators, inference, traits, per_parent rows, fk affinity matching, state machines, SQL derivations (real lineage), views, and logged imperfections. Keys starting with '_' are comments and are stripped before validation.

vocab._note: Per-category product pools so names, categories, storage zones, and shelf life stay mutually coherent.

tables.name: application
tables.purpose: Application catalog for the fictional landscape.

tables._note: CRM is the customer system of record. created_at is sorted so account IDs correlate with onboarding date, like a real sequence-keyed table.
tables.name: account
tables.purpose: CRM customer accounts (restaurants, institutions, chains).

columns.name: account_name
columns._note: Chains draw from a small shared brand pool with numbered locations ('Cedar Point Cantina #3'); independents/institutions get unique one-off names.

columns.name: email
columns._note: Role/proprietor addresses on the account's own fictional subdomain (orders@cedar-point-cantina.example.com) — all-personal emails on a B2B account master are a tell.

columns.name: created_at
columns._note: sorted: account IDs correlate with onboarding order; backfill: 65% of accounts predate the data window (tenured book), so in-window volume grows ~7%/yr instead of ramping from zero.

tables._note: ERP customer is generated 1:1 per CRM account (parent_copy keeps attributes coherent across systems); imperfections later make the two systems disagree realistically.
tables.name: customer
tables.purpose: ERP customer master, fed from CRM but maintained separately.

tables.name: product
tables.purpose: Product master with cost and tiered list pricing.

columns.name: product_name
columns._note: category is drawn first, then the name comes from that category's pool — names, zones, and shelf life stay coherent.

tables._note: Exactly 4 warehouses, one per region, so fk affinity matching on region has full coverage.
tables.name: warehouse
tables.purpose: Distribution centers.

tables.name: contract
tables.purpose: Customer pricing contracts (chains and institutions).

tables.name: contract_line
tables.purpose: Contracted price per product; discount off list.

tables._note: Orders flow through a right-censored state machine: orders near the as_of date are legitimately stuck mid-pipeline. Warehouse assignment uses affinity matching on region with a 5% leak.
tables.name: sales_order
tables.purpose: Sales order headers.

rows._note: Volume conditioned on segment (chains order multiples of independents) plus a heavy-tailed per-account activity weight so a whale tier emerges.

columns.name: order_date
columns._note: Calendar-weighted date bounded by the customer's onboarding date: weekday/seasonality shape AND no orders before the customer existed. Use date_offset only for short process lags, never long activity windows (clamping piles rows onto the as_of date).

tables.name: sales_order_event
tables.purpose: Order status history emitted by the state machine.
tables.grain: one row per order per state entered

tables.name: sales_order_line
tables.purpose: Order lines priced from the product list with contract/street mix.
tables.grain: one row per order per line number

columns.name: product_id
columns._note: zipf(0.5): popular SKUs lead but the top item stays ~2-4% of lines, not 20%.

tables.name: inventory_lot
tables.purpose: Received lots with expiry for perishables.

columns.name: expiry_date
columns._note: Shelf life conditioned on category: produce days, frozen months.

tables.name: driver
tables.purpose: Delivery drivers per DC.

tables._note: Routes and stops are DERIVED from shipped orders so delivery dates, warehouses, and order statuses are coherent by construction — never wire delivery events to orders with a random FK.
tables.name: route
tables.purpose: Daily delivery routes per DC, one per warehouse per delivery day.
tables.grain: one row per warehouse per delivery date

tables.name: delivery_stop
tables.purpose: Stops with proof-of-delivery notes, derived from shipped orders.

tables.name: case
tables.purpose: Exception and review queues.

tables.name: case_event
tables.purpose: Case status history.

tables.name: change_event
tables.purpose: Field-level change history on CRM accounts.

columns.name: old_value
columns._note: Old/new values must be plausible for the changed field — random strings here are the first thing a reviewer notices.

tables.name: job_run
tables.purpose: Batch job run log.

tables.name: rule
tables.purpose: Data quality rule catalog (results come from the dq.rule_result_current view).

tables._note: Everything below is populated by SQL derivations from the source tables above — real lineage, not parallel-faked data.
tables.name: crm_account_extract
tables.purpose: Nightly CRM extract as landed: all text, US-format dates.

tables.name: crm_account
tables.purpose: Cleaned CRM accounts; normalizes raw extract dates.

tables.name: entity_identifier
tables.purpose: Source-system identifier crosswalk for canonical customers.

tables.name: customer
tables.purpose: Canonical customer joining CRM and ERP through the crosswalk.

tables.name: product
tables.purpose: Canonical product.

tables.name: invoice
tables.purpose: Invoices derived from invoiced orders (1:1 with order).

tables.name: invoice_line
tables.purpose: Invoice lines copied from order lines at invoicing.
tables.grain: one row per invoice per line

tables.name: payment
tables.purpose: Payments against paid invoices.

tables.name: dim_date
tables.purpose: Calendar dimension.

tables.name: dim_customer
tables.purpose: Customer dimension from the canonical layer.

tables.name: dim_product
tables.purpose: Product dimension.

tables.name: fact_order_line
tables.purpose: Order line fact.
tables.grain: one row per sales order line

tables.name: fact_invoice_line
tables.purpose: Invoice line fact.

derivations.name: raw_crm_account_extract
derivations._note: Raw landing copy with US-format dates; format_drift imperfection later corrupts a slice of these, which stg parsing then turns into NULLs that DQ-004 catches.
sql: select cast(account_id as text), account_name, segment, sales_region, city, state, status,

derivations.name: stg_crm_account
sql: select cast(account_id as integer), trim(account_name), lower(segment), sales_region, city, state, lower(status),

derivations.name: xref_entity_identifier
derivations._note: A crosswalk that maps a single system is not a crosswalk — include both CRM and ERP identifiers.
sql: select row_number() over (order by c.customer_id), 'customer', c.crm_account_id,
'LedgerWorks ERP', 'customer', cast(c.customer_id as text), date(c.created_at), null, 1
select 100000 + row_number() over (order by a.account_id), 'customer', a.account_id,

derivations.name: core_customer
sql: select s.account_id, s.account_name, s.segment, s.sales_region, s.city, s.state, s.status,
(select cast(x.source_record_id as integer) from xref.entity_identifier x
where x.canonical_entity_id = s.account_id and x.source_system = 'LedgerWorks ERP'

derivations.name: core_product
sql: select product_id, sku, product_name, category, storage_zone, list_price, active_flag

derivations.name: erp_invoice
derivations._note: due_date honors each customer's credit terms (COD/NET14/.../NET60) and aged invoices flow through a collections lifecycle instead of sitting 'open' forever.
sql: select o.order_id, o.customer_id, date(o.invoiced_at) as invoice_date, o.status,
date(o.invoiced_at, '+' || case c.credit_terms when 'COD' then 0 when 'NET14' then 14
when 'NET45' then 45 when 'NET60' then 60 else 30 end || ' days') as due_date
from erp.sales_order o join erp.customer c on c.customer_id = o.customer_id
select b.order_id, 'INV-' || printf('%07d', b.order_id), b.customer_id, b.invoice_date, b.due_date,
(select round(sum(l.extended_amount), 2) from erp.sales_order_line l where l.order_id = b.order_id),
when julianday('2025-12-31') - julianday(b.due_date) > 120 and b.order_id % 9 = 0 then 'written_off'

derivations.name: erp_invoice_line
sql: select l.order_line_id, i.invoice_id, l.line_number, l.product_id, l.quantity, l.unit_price, l.extended_amount, null
from erp.sales_order_line l join erp.invoice i on i.invoice_id = l.order_id;

derivations.name: erp_payment
derivations._note: Real AR is imperfect: ~4% pay in two installments, ~3% short-pay by cents-to-dollars. Exact 1:1 full payments for every invoice is a synthetic tell.
sql: select i.invoice_id, i.invoice_total, date(o.paid_at) as pay_date
from erp.invoice i join erp.sales_order o on o.order_id = i.invoice_id
case when invoice_id % 10 < 5 then 'ACH' when invoice_id % 10 < 8 then 'check' else 'card' end,
case when invoice_id % 23 = 0 then 'Partial payment 1 of 2.'
when invoice_id % 31 = 0 then 'Short pay, freight deduction.' else null end
select 'PAY-' || printf('%07d', p.invoice_id) || '-2', p.invoice_id, date(p.pay_date, '+12 days'),
from (select i.invoice_id, i.invoice_total, date(o.paid_at) as pay_date
from erp.invoice i join erp.sales_order o on o.order_id = i.invoice_id
where o.paid_at is not null and i.invoice_id % 23 = 0) p;

derivations.name: tms_route
sql: select distinct o.warehouse_id, date(o.shipped_at, '+1 day') as route_date
where o.shipped_at is not null and date(o.shipped_at, '+1 day') <= '2025-12-31'
row_number() over (partition by warehouse_id order by driver_id) - 1 as rk,
count(*) over (partition by warehouse_id) as cnt
select row_number() over (order by s.warehouse_id, s.route_date),
and d.rk = cast(strftime('%j', s.route_date) as integer) % d.cnt;

derivations.name: tms_delivery_stop
sql: select o.order_id, o.warehouse_id, date(o.shipped_at, '+1 day') as delivery_date
where o.shipped_at is not null and date(o.shipped_at, '+1 day') <= '2025-12-31'
row_number() over (partition by r.route_id order by s.order_id) as stop_sequence
join tms.route r on r.warehouse_id = s.warehouse_id and r.route_date = s.delivery_date
select row_number() over (order by route_id, stop_sequence),
case when order_id % 7 = 0 then 'Signed by receiver.'
when order_id % 7 = 1 then 'Left at loading dock.'
when order_id % 7 = 4 then 'Short one case, credit requested.'

derivations.name: wh_dim_date
sql: union all select date(dt, '+1 day') from d where dt < '2025-12-31'
select dt, cast(strftime('%Y', dt) as integer), cast(strftime('%m', dt) as integer),
cast(strftime('%d', dt) as integer), cast(strftime('%w', dt) as integer),
case when strftime('%w', dt) in ('0', '6') then 1 else 0 end

derivations.name: wh_dim_customer
sql: select customer_uid, account_name, segment, sales_region, status from core.customer;

derivations.name: wh_dim_product
sql: select product_uid, sku, product_name, category from core.product;

derivations.name: wh_fact_order_line
sql: select l.order_line_id, o.order_id, o.order_date, o.crm_account_id, l.product_id,
o.warehouse_id, o.channel, l.quantity, l.unit_price, l.extended_amount, o.status
join erp.sales_order o on o.order_id = l.order_id;

derivations.name: wh_fact_invoice_line
sql: select l.invoice_line_id, l.invoice_id, i.invoice_date, i.customer_id, l.product_id,
from erp.invoice_line l join erp.invoice i on i.invoice_id = l.invoice_id;

derivations.name: mart_sales_daily
sql: select order_date, count(distinct order_id) as orders, sum(quantity) as units,

derivations.name: mart_ar_aging
sql: select i.invoice_id, i.invoice_number, i.customer_id, i.invoice_date, i.due_date, i.invoice_total,
cast(julianday('2025-12-31') - julianday(i.due_date) as integer) as days_past_due,
from erp.invoice i where i.invoice_status not in ('paid', 'written_off');

derivations.name: control_recon_order_invoice
derivations._note: Reconciliation as a VIEW so it reflects post-derivation imperfections (restatement reversals on invoice lines create the breaks).
sql: select ol.order_id, round(ol.total, 2) as order_total, round(il.total, 2) as invoice_total,
from (select order_id, sum(extended_amount) as total from erp.sales_order_line group by order_id) ol
join (select i.invoice_id as order_id, sum(l.extended_amount) as total
from erp.invoice_line l join erp.invoice i on i.invoice_id = l.invoice_id
group by i.invoice_id) il on il.order_id = ol.order_id

derivations.name: dq_rule_result_current
sql: select 'DQ-001' as rule_code, 'active account missing email' as issue, cast(account_id as text) as entity_id
from crm.account where status = 'active' and (email is null or email = '')
select 'DQ-002', 'order line references unknown product', cast(order_line_id as text)
where not exists (select 1 from erp.product p where p.product_id = l.product_id)
select 'DQ-003', 'erp customer missing xref mapping', cast(c.customer_id as text)
where not exists (select 1 from xref.entity_identifier x
where x.source_system = 'LedgerWorks ERP' and x.source_record_id = cast(c.customer_id as text))
from raw.crm_account_extract where source_created_date not like '__/__/____';

imperfections.name: crm_duplicate_accounts
imperfections.scenario: Reps create duplicate accounts; MDM review queue exists for this.

imperfections.name: orphan_order_lines
imperfections.scenario: Discontinued products hard-deleted from the product master.

imperfections.name: order_manual_overrides
imperfections.scenario: CSRs override pricing/terms with supervisor approval.

imperfections.name: raw_date_format_drift
imperfections.scenario: Legacy extract job pads dates differently after a patch.

imperfections.name: missing_customer_xref
imperfections.scenario: Mappings lost during the 2024 integration migration.

imperfections.name: stg_name_conflicts
imperfections.scenario: Staging dedup logic mangles some names vs source.

imperfections.name: invoice_line_restatements
imperfections.scenario: Billing corrections issued as reversal + restated pairs; these create the order-vs-invoice recon breaks.

imperfections.name: duplicate_job_runs
imperfections.scenario: Scheduler retries log duplicate run records.

controls.rule_name: Order lines tie to invoice lines

dataflows.name: customer_mastering
dataflows.sequence: crm.account -> raw extract -> staging -> xref -> core.customer -> wh.dim_customer

dataflows.name: order_to_cash
dataflows.sequence: erp.sales_order -> state machine -> erp.invoice/invoice_line -> erp.payment -> wh facts -> marts
